title: View-Compose Interop & Adaptive Layouts
description: ComposeView, AndroidView, ViewCompositionStrategy, WindowSizeClass, foldables, and predictive back
lesson:
Migrating to Compose

Most real-world apps aren't greenfield — they have existing View-based code. Understanding interop patterns is essential for senior engineers managing migrations.

Views in Compose: AndroidView

@Composable fun MapScreen() { AndroidView( factory = { context -> MapView(context).apply { onCreate(null) getMapAsync { map -> map.moveCamera(CameraUpdateFactory.newLatLngZoom(nyc, 12f)) } } }, update = { mapView -> // Called on every recomposition — update the view here }, modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize() ) }
AndroidViewBinding works the same way but with ViewBinding — use it for inflating XML layouts inside Compose.

Compose in Views: ComposeView

// In a Fragment class ProfileFragment : Fragment() { override fun onCreateView( inflater: LayoutInflater, container: ViewGroup?, savedInstanceState: Bundle? ): View { return ComposeView(requireContext()).apply { setViewCompositionStrategy( ViewCompositionStrategy.DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed ) setContent { MaterialTheme { ProfileScreen() } } } } }

ViewCompositionStrategy

Controls when Compose disposes its composition (releases resources):

StrategyDisposes whenUse case
DisposeOnDetachedFromWindowView detaches from windowDefault. Activities, simple views
DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyedFragment's view lifecycle endsFragments — handles view/fragment lifecycle mismatch
DisposeOnLifecycleDestroyedSpecific lifecycle destroyedDialogs, custom lifecycle owners
Fragment gotcha: Without DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed, Compose may leak resources because Fragment's view can be destroyed while the Fragment itself lives on (e.g., on the back stack).

State Bridging

// LiveData → Compose State val users by viewModel.users.observeAsState(initial = emptyList()) // Flow → Compose State val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsState() // Compose State → LiveData (less common, for interop) val liveData = snapshotFlow { composeState.value }.asLiveData()

Migration Strategy

Google recommends bottom-up migration:

  1. Start with leaf composables (buttons, cards, list items)
  2. Move up to screen-level composables
  3. Replace Fragments with full Compose screens
  4. Replace Navigation Component with Navigation Compose

Adaptive Layouts

WindowSizeClass

@Composable fun MyApp() { val windowSizeClass = calculateWindowSizeClass(this) when (windowSizeClass.widthSizeClass) { WindowWidthSizeClass.Compact -> PhoneLayout() // < 600dp — phone WindowWidthSizeClass.Medium -> TabletLayout() // 600-840dp — small tablet, foldable WindowWidthSizeClass.Expanded -> DesktopLayout() // > 840dp — large tablet, desktop } }

NavigationSuiteScaffold

Automatically switches between bottom navigation (phone), navigation rail (tablet), and navigation drawer (desktop) based on window size.

Predictive Back Gesture

Mandatory in Android 15+. Shows a preview of the previous screen as the user swipes back:

// Enable in manifest (required for Android 15+) <application android:enableOnBackInvokedCallback="true"> // In Compose — handle custom back logic @Composable fun EditScreen(onBack: () -> Unit) { var showDialog by remember { mutableStateOf(false) } BackHandler(enabled = hasUnsavedChanges) { showDialog = true // Show "discard changes?" dialog } }

questions.title: ViewCompositionStrategy in Fragments
questions.description: Why should you use DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed when using ComposeView inside a Fragment?
options: It makes composition faster by caching the view tree
Fragments have separate view and fragment lifecycles — without it, Compose may not dispose when the Fragment's view is destroyed (e.g., on the back stack), causing resource leaks
It enables predictive back gesture support in the ComposeView
It's required for AndroidView interop to work correctly
questions.explanation: Fragments have a subtle lifecycle issue: the Fragment's view can be destroyed while the Fragment itself remains alive (e.g., when added to the back stack). The default strategy (DisposeOnDetachedFromWindow) may not align with this. DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed ties disposal to the Fragment's view lifecycle, ensuring proper cleanup.
hints: Fragment has TWO lifecycles — the fragment itself and its view
What happens when a Fragment goes on the back stack?

questions.title: AndroidView Factory
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to embed a traditional WebView inside a Compose screen.

blanks.hint: Compose function that wraps Android Views
Called on every recomposition to update the View

questions.explanation: AndroidView bridges traditional Android Views into Compose. factory creates the view once. update runs on each recomposition — use it to push new state to the view. The view's lifecycle is managed by Compose's composition lifecycle.
hints: factory is like onCreate — runs once
update is like onBind — runs with new data

questions.title: State Bridging
questions.description: What does observeAsState do in this code?
options: It converts the Compose state back to LiveData for the ViewModel
It creates a snapshot of the LiveData that never updates
It converts LiveData to Compose State, automatically triggering recomposition when LiveData emits new values
It observes the LiveData on a background thread
questions.explanation: observeAsState() bridges LiveData into Compose's state system. It subscribes to the LiveData lifecycle-aware and converts each emission into a Compose State. When LiveData posts a new value, the State updates and triggers recomposition. initial provides the value before the first emission. The equivalent for Flow is collectAsState().
hints: It's a bridge between two reactive systems: LiveData and Compose State

questions.title: WindowSizeClass
questions.description: Which WindowWidthSizeClass breakpoint represents a typical phone in portrait mode?
options: Compact (< 600dp)
Medium (600-840dp)
Expanded (> 840dp)
WindowSizeClass doesn't have predefined breakpoints
questions.explanation: Compact (< 600dp) = phones in portrait. Medium (600-840dp) = small tablets, foldables unfolded, phones in landscape. Expanded (> 840dp) = large tablets, desktop. These replace hardcoded device-type checks. Use them to choose between canonical layouts (single-pane vs list-detail vs supporting pane).
hints: Most phones are under 600dp wide in portrait

questions.title: ComposeView Leak in Fragment
questions.description: This Fragment leaks Compose resources when placed on the back stack. Fix it.
questions.explanation: When a Fragment goes on the back stack, its view is destroyed but the Fragment lives on. Without DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed, the Compose composition stays alive, holding references to views and resources. This strategy disposes the composition when the Fragment's view lifecycle is destroyed, matching the Fragment transaction behavior.
hints: What happens to a Fragment's view when another Fragment is pushed on top?
The default strategy doesn't handle Fragment back stack correctly

questions.title: Adaptive Layout
questions.description: Write a composable that shows a single-pane list on phones and a list-detail layout on tablets, using WindowSizeClass.

checks.message: Use layout composables for the split view

questions.explanation: The list-detail canonical layout uses WindowSizeClass to choose between a single-pane (Compact) and two-pane (Medium/Expanded) layout. On tablets, Row with weight modifiers creates the split. On phones, only the list shows — tapping navigates to a separate detail screen. This is one of Google's three canonical layouts (list-detail, supporting pane, feed).
hints: Row with weight() modifiers creates the split view
On Compact, only show the list — detail is a separate screen

questions.title: Predictive Back Gesture
questions.description: What must you do to support predictive back gesture in Android 15+?
options: Override onBackPressed() in every Activity
Set android:enableOnBackInvokedCallback="true" in the manifest and use BackHandler or OnBackInvokedCallback instead of onBackPressed()
Add a SwipeRefreshLayout to each screen
Nothing — it's automatic in all apps
questions.explanation: Predictive back requires opting in via android:enableOnBackInvokedCallback="true" in the manifest. Then use BackHandler (Compose) or OnBackInvokedCallback (Views) instead of the deprecated onBackPressed(). The system previews the previous screen as the user swipes. Legacy onBackPressed() breaks the preview animation.
hints: onBackPressed() is deprecated — the new system needs to know about back handling ahead of time

questions.title: Migration Strategy
questions.description: Google recommends which migration strategy for adopting Compose in an existing View-based app?
options: Top-down: replace Activities and Fragments first, then individual views
Bottom-up: start with leaf composables (buttons, cards), then screens, then navigation
Full rewrite: pause features and convert everything at once
Side-by-side: maintain both View and Compose versions permanently
questions.explanation: Bottom-up migration is recommended: start with small, leaf UI components (buttons, list items, cards), then compose entire screens, then replace Fragment/Activity navigation with Navigation Compose. This approach is incremental, low-risk, and lets the team learn Compose gradually while shipping features.
hints: Which approach lets you ship features while migrating gradually?
